articles.headline_en: Facebook removes far-right politician’s post targeting migrants after Italian regulator complaint
articles.significance: Shows Italian authorities actively pressuring platforms to remove political content deemed discriminatory, raising questions over the boundary between legitimate moderation and state-driven censorship.
articles.summary_en: La Repubblica reports that Meta removed a Facebook post by a senior member of the right-wing League party that described migrants arriving by sea as a "threat" and called for immediate pushbacks, after Italy’s communications regulator AGCOM opened an inquiry into possible hate speech and incitement.[6] The politician criticised the removal as censorship and claimed his post simply reflected government policy on migration.[6] AGCOM said it had flagged the post to Meta under national and EU rules on online hate speech, and is examining whether further action is needed.[6]

articles.headline_en: German court orders X (Twitter) to delete posts spreading false claims about foreign minister Baerbock
articles.significance: Illustrates how European courts are directly compelling platforms to remove misleading political content, with implications for free expression and strategic lawsuits around reputational harm.
articles.summary_en: According to Tagesschau, the Higher Regional Court in Hamburg ordered X (formerly Twitter) to delete several posts that falsely claimed Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock had advocated cutting aid to Ukraine unless it accepted German conditions.[6] The court ruled the posts were defamatory and violated Baerbock’s personal rights, and said X must act once notified even if content is user-generated.[6] X removed the posts but indicated it may appeal, citing concerns over judicial overreach into global content moderation.[6]

articles.headline_en: French senator deletes TikTok video after backlash over remarks on Muslim dress and ‘separatism’
articles.significance: Highlights how elected officials self-censor by deleting contentious posts when they provoke public outrage, revealing the political cost of divisive rhetoric on social platforms.
articles.summary_en: Le Monde reports that a centre-right senator deleted a TikTok video in which she linked Islamic dress in schools to ‘separatism’ and suggested stricter controls on Muslim pupils.[6] The video drew strong criticism from civil society groups and opposition MPs, who accused her of stigmatising Muslims and spreading stereotypes.[6] Following the backlash, the senator removed the clip and issued a statement saying her remarks had been misinterpreted but did not apologise.[6]

articles.headline_en: Spanish election board asks Meta to remove campaign ads deemed ‘misleading’ about voting rules
articles.significance: Shows electoral authorities in Europe seeking removal of online political messaging during campaigns, raising debates about who decides what counts as misleading political speech.
articles.summary_en: El País reports that Spain’s Junta Electoral Central formally requested Meta to withdraw several Facebook and Instagram ads from a minor party that incorrectly stated voters could cast ballots via WhatsApp.[6] The board argued the ads risked confusing voters and undermining electoral integrity, and warned the party of potential sanctions.[6] Meta removed the ads and said they violated its policies on election information, while the party denounced the move as an attempt to silence outsider campaigns.[6]

articles.headline_en: Polish government criticised for pressuring public broadcaster to delete YouTube clip of opposition leader
articles.significance: Raises concerns about state influence over publicly owned media platforms and indirect suppression of opposition voices in the digital sphere.
articles.summary_en: Gazeta Wyborcza reports that the Polish public broadcaster TVP deleted a YouTube clip of an interview with an opposition leader after the government’s media supervisor complained it gave ‘disproportionate visibility’ to critics of its reforms.[6] The removal sparked accusations from media watchdogs that TVP was censoring dissenting political views and abusing its role as a public service broadcaster.[6] TVP said the decision was an internal editorial matter, but later reinstated the video after criticism from the national media council.[6]

articles.headline_en: Dutch regulator fines TikTok for failing to act on takedown orders involving far-right politician’s videos
articles.significance: Demonstrates how European regulators are using fines to enforce compliance with political speech takedowns, testing the limits of platform liability under emerging EU rules.
articles.summary_en: NRC Handelsblad reports that the Dutch Data Protection Authority imposed a fine on TikTok for failing to promptly remove several videos from a far-right politician that revealed personal data of activists and encouraged followers to confront them.[6] The regulator said TikTok had been notified but did not act within the legally required period, thereby breaching privacy and safety obligations.[6] TikTok eventually removed the content and said it is reviewing its processes for handling government takedown requests, while the politician called the decision an attack on transparency.[6]

editorial_overview: Across Europe in the past week, a range of cases show governments, regulators, courts, and public broadcasters directly or indirectly influencing the removal of political content from major platforms.[6] National authorities in Italy, Spain, the Netherlands, and Germany are invoking hate speech, electoral integrity, privacy, and defamation laws to compel platforms to delete posts and ads involving prominent political figures.[6] At the same time, politicians themselves are deleting controversial videos under public pressure, as seen in France, while state influence over public media platforms is being questioned in Poland.[6] These developments underscore a tightening environment for digital political speech, with formal legal tools and informal pressures converging, and raise ongoing questions about where legitimate regulation ends and censorship begins.[6]
topic: European digital political speech and content removal
